verbatim: I have not been on bail, nor have I been kept in confinement. This was not customary in a case of ἔνδειξις. The accused, if a citizen, was usually given the choice of furnishing sureties (ἐγγυηταί) or suffering imprisonment until the case came into court.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text and its commentary establish that Athenian citizens facing certain legal charges had the right to provide sureties to remain free before their trial, rather than being imprisoned.

verbatim: Grant immunity The word ἄδεια is used in two slightly different senses. (a) It is the immunity granted by the Assembly or Council to persons who have a statement to make to them, but who are debarred from addressing them without special permission. This applied to slaves, metics, and women.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under normal circumstances, these groups are barred from the political assembly. However, the legal mechanism of 'adeia' (immunity) allowed them to speak if they had information regarding a crime against the state.

verbatim: so the Prytanes decided to clear the meeting of non-initiates and themselves fetch the lad indicated by Pythonicus. They went off, and returned with a slave belonging to Archebiades, son of Polemarchus.
extracted_rules.reasoning: When the Assembly discussed matters pertaining to the secret Mysteries, those who had not been initiated into the cult were forcibly excluded from the physical meeting space.

verbatim: whereupon my father furnished surieties and brought an action against Speusippus for making an illegal proposal... He then blocked Speusippus’ proposal by a γραφὴ παρανόμων which had to be settled before the proposal could take effect.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Athenian law allowed citizens to halt a proposed decree by filing a 'graphe paranomon' (suit against illegal proposals), which forced a trial before the jury to determine the legality of the proposal.

verbatim: because the law ran that whereas an informer’s claim to immunity should be allowed if his information were true, he should be put to death, if it were not. Yet if there is one thing of which you are all certain, it is the fact that my father and I both escaped with our lives. That could not have happened, if I had informed against my father: either he or I would have had to die.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a standing law where informers are granted immunity if their information is verified, whereas they face the death penalty if it is false. This is a general rule within the Athenian legal system during this period.

verbatim: and he offered the defence the opportunity of examining his slaves under torture; those who were ready to hand over their slaves, he said, ought not to meet with a refusal of the test which they were proposing, when those who were not ready to hand them over were forced to do so. You all know my father’s challenge to be a fact.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes the legal convention of 'basanos', where slave evidence was only admissible when obtained through torture, and masters could be forced to hand slaves over for this purpose.

verbatim: It was therefore publicly resolved that such members of the court of the Thesmothetae as were initiates should be presented with the informations of the several claimants and decide between them. ... So much for the profanation of the Mysteries, gentlemen, on which the information lodged against me is based and which you are here as initiates to investigate.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In cases involving the profanation of the Mysteries, the right to sit as a juror was restricted to those citizens who had been initiated into the cult themselves.

verbatim: Should Cephisius here, who was responsible for the information laid against me, fail to gain one-fifth of your votes and so lose his rights as a citizen, he is forbidden to set foot within the sanctuary of the Two Goddesses under pain of death. The prosecutor who failed to gain one-fifth of the votes of the jury was condemned to a fine of one thousand drachmae and debarred from bringing a similar action in the future.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Under Athenian law, a prosecutor who failed to secure 20% of the jury's votes faced partial atimia (loss of rights), including a fine and the loss of the right to bring future lawsuits.

verbatim: Should Cephisius here, who was responsible for the information laid against me, fail to gain one-fifth of your votes and so lose his rights as a citizen, he is forbidden to set foot within the sanctuary of the Two Goddesses under pain of death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In religious cases (asebeia), an accuser who fails to meet the required vote threshold is specifically barred from the temple related to the charge, on pain of death.

verbatim: Peisander hereupon rose and moved that the decree passed in the archonship of Scamandrius be suspended and all whose names were on the list sent to the wheel, to ensure the discovery of everyone concerned before nightfall. The Council broke into shouts of approval. At that Mantitheus and Apsephion took sanctuary on the hearth, and appealed to be allowed to furnish sureties and stand trial, instead of being racked.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text mentions a specific decree (the decree of Scamandrius) that protected Athenian citizens from being tortured ('racked' or 'sent to the wheel'). The motion to suspend it during a crisis confirms that under normal legal circumstances, this protection was a standard right for citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain free pending trial
verbatim: At that Mantitheus and Apsephion took sanctuary on the hearth, and appealed to be allowed to furnish sureties and stand trial, instead of being racked. They finally managed to gain their request; but no sooner had they provided their sureties than they leapt on horseback and deserted to the enemy, leaving the sureties to their fate, as they were now liable to the same penalties as the prisoners for whom they had gone bail.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage describes an established legal mechanism where accused citizens could provide 'sureties' (financial/legal guarantors) to avoid immediate imprisonment or torture while awaiting trial.

verbatim: After the fall of the Four Hundred Peisander fled to Decelea; he was condemned to death in absentia and his property was confiscated.
Am I to look on while my own kindred perish for a crime which they have not committed: while they themselves are put to death and their goods are confiscated
extracted_rules.reasoning: In Athens, capital condemnation (whether in person or in absentia) was legally coupled with the seizure of the individual's material assets by the state.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain one's own earnings
verbatim: Diocleides’ tale was that he had had to fetch the earnings of a slave of his at Laurium. The mines of Laurium in S. Attica were leased by the state to private individuals. These in their turn hired slaves to work them, if they had not enough of their own. The slave’s earnings were paid to his master.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly states that the wages or earnings produced by a slave (specifically in the mining sector) were legally and conventionally the property of the master, not the individual performing the labor.

verbatim: On his return to Athens he found a commission already appointed to investigate, and a reward of one hundred minae offered for information; so seeing Euphemus, the brother of Callias, son of Telocles, sitting in his smithy, he took him to the temple of Hephaestus. Then... he said that he would rather take our money than the state’s
extracted_rules.reasoning: The Athenian state used public treasury funds to incentivize citizens to provide testimony or information regarding public outrages (the mutilation of the Hermae).

verbatim: Isotimides proposed to exclude from temples all who had committed an act of impiety and admitted their guilt. I have done neither: I have not committed any act of impiety, nor have I admitted guilt.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites the decree of Isotimides, a specific legal rule in Athens that restricted individuals from entering religious sites if they had confessed to acts of impiety.

verbatim: all who farmed taxes and then defaulted or were liable to the state as sureties for a defaulter, had to pay within eight Prytanies; otherwise, the sum due was doubled and the delinquent’s property distrained upon.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the legal consequences for tax farmers and their sureties who fail to meet financial obligations to the state, specifically the loss of property rights through distraint.

verbatim: Similarly, all who deserted on the field of battle, who were found guilty of evasion of military service, of cowardice, or of withholding a ship from action, ... were deprived of their personal rights, while retaining possession of their property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Individuals who failed in their military duties were subject to atimia (loss of civic rights), a general rule governing the boundaries of active citizenship.

verbatim: or who were found guilty of maltreating their parents, were deprived of their personal rights, while retaining possession of their property.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The law of Athens mandated atimia for citizens who failed in their familial duties toward their parents, stripping them of political and personal rights.

verbatim: The soldiers who remained in Athens under the Four Hundred are a case in point. They enjoyed all the rights of ordinary citizens, except that they were forbidden to speak in the Assembly or become members of the Council.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the restoration of democracy, a specific group of soldiers associated with the previous oligarchic coup (the Four Hundred) were targeted with partial disfranchisement.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to vote in the Assembly
verbatim: whereas the Athenians have enacted that persons disfranchised and public debtors may speak and propose measures in the Assembly with impunity, the People shall pass the decree which was passed at the time of the Persian Wars and which proved of benefit to Athens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a specific decree (of Patrocleides) that restores the right to speak and propose measures (political rights) to citizens who had previously been disfranchised or were in debt to the state.

verbatim: No law shall be directed against an individual without applying to all citizens alike, unless an Assembly of six thousand so resolve by secret ballot. A reference to ostracism.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage cites a general law that prohibits the passage of laws targeting specific individuals (privilegium) rather than the whole body of citizens, except under the specific high-threshold procedure of an assembly of 6000.

verbatim: In no circumstances shall magistrates enforce a law which has not been inscribed. No decree, whether of the Council or Assembly, shall override a law.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the restoration of democracy, a rule was established that only laws formally inscribed and codified could be enforced by magistrates, protecting citizens from obscure or unrecorded decrees.

verbatim: All decisions given in private suits and by arbitrators under the democracy shall be valid. ... you did so to avoid the cancelling of debts and the reopening of such suits, and to ensure the enforcement of private contracts.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explains a rule maintaining the validity of past judicial decisions in private matters to ensure social and economic stability (debt collection and contract enforcement) for citizens.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from prosecution for past events
verbatim: and I will allow no information or arrest arising out of past events, save only in the case of those who fled from Athens. ... you enacted that only such laws should be enforced as had been passed since the archonship of Eucleides.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the amnesty rules where citizens were protected from prosecution for public offenses or arrests linked to events before the archonship of Eucleides (the restoration of democracy).

verbatim: it being laid down by law that any defaulting tax farmer may be so punished by the Council—he retired into exile. ... obtain thereby a return of ninety minae from the farmers occupying the lands concerned. He then defaulted; and since he would have been placed in close confinement had he appeared in Athens.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites a specific legal rule where those who purchase the right to collect taxes (tax farmers) and default on their payment to the state are subject to summary imprisonment by the Council.

verbatim: Whosoever shall hold a public office after the suppression of the democracy may be slain with impunity. No taint shall rest upon his slayer, and he shall possess the goods of the slain. [§97] If anyone shall suppress the democracy at Athens or hold public office after its suppression, he shall become a public enemy and be slain with impunity; his goods shall be confiscated and a tithe given to the Goddess.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text cites a specific Athenian decree (of Demophantus) establishing that those who serve in office after the democracy is overthrown lose their legal protection against being killed and forfeit their property to the state and the slayer.

extracted_rules.rule: Death penalty for Eleusinium bough placement
verbatim: They allege that it was I who placed it in the Eleusinium, and that under ancient law the penalty for doing such a thing during the Mysteries is death. [§116] Callias rose once more and said that under an ancient law, as officially interpreted on a former occasion by his father, Hipponicus, the penalty for placing a bough in the Eleusinium during the Mysteries was instant death.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Andocides describes an 'ancient law' of Athens that makes placing a suppliant's bough in the Eleusinium during the performance of the Mysteries a capital offense with mandated instant death.

extracted_rules.resource: Exemption from prosecution under expired laws
verbatim: Well, Mr. Informer, is this law in force? Yes or no, you practised villain? No; and the reason for that is of course that only laws passed after the archonship of Eucleides can be applied.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the Athenian legal reform after 403 BCE (the archonship of Eucleides), where citizens were protected from being prosecuted under laws or decrees that were not reenacted or passed after that date.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold property and reside in Athens
verbatim: For their sakes you removed stones of record, annulled laws, and cancelled decrees; and it is because they trust you that they are still in Athens, gentlemen. [§110] by deciding to restore your exiles and give back their rights to the citizens who had lost them you showed that you still had the noble spirit of your forefathers.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Following the reconciliation after the Thirty Tyrants, Athens passed measures to restore citizenship rights and property to those previously exiled or disfranchised, ensuring they could live in the city as full citizens.

verbatim: Callias, you impious scoundrel, first you are giving interpretations, when you have no right to do such a thing as a member of the Ceryces. ἐξήγησις was the prerogative of Eulmopidae alone.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Andocides identifies a specific religious legal rule where only the clan of the Eumolpidae has the right to provide official interpretations (exegesis) of sacred law, specifically excluding other groups like the Ceryces.

extracted_rules.rule: Heiress marriage to next of kin
extracted_rules.resource: Right to inherit property through marriage
verbatim: He died in Sicily without male issue, but left two daughters who ought now to have passed to Leagrus and myself. If a citizen died intestate, leaving daughters, but no sons, the daughters became heiresses ( ἐπίκληροι) and shared the estate between them. They were then obliged by law to marry their nearest male relatives, but not in the ascending line.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the Athenian legal institution of the epikleros, where daughters without brothers are legally bound to marry the nearest male relative to ensure the estate remains within the lineage.

verbatim: At that, the woman’s relatives came to the altar at the Apaturia with the child and a victim for sacrifice, and told Callias to begin the rites. . . the Ceryces voted in favour of the law which they have, whereby a father can introduce his son, if he swears that it is his own son whom he is introducing. So Callias took hold of the altar and swore that the boy was his legitimate son by Chrysilla.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text outlines the procedure at the Apaturia festival where a father exercises his legal right and obligation to register a son into the phratry by swearing an oath of legitimacy.

verbatim: Yet so far from prosecuting, they actually proposed me for public services, first as Gymnasiarch at the Hephaestia, then as head of the state deputation to the Isthmus and to Olympia, and finally as Treasurer of the Sacred Monies on the Acropolis.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Andocides lists the various liturgies (public services) he was expected to perform and fund, which were legal obligations imposed on citizens of a certain wealth bracket in Athens.

verbatim: Now since Athens as a city was spared because of the brave exploits of your forefathers, I likewise claim to be spared because of the brave deeds of mine; for my own forefathers themselves played no small part in those very exploits to which Athens owed her salvation, and I therefore have the right to expect from you the mercy shown to you yourselves by the Greeks.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Andocides argues that his lineage provides him a specific right to legal mercy ('mercy shown to you') and protection from execution, based on the historical services rendered to the state by his ancestors. This reflects a norm where the merit of a noble family (lineage) functions as a resource for descendants during legal trials.

verbatim: When you are ready to extend civic rights to Thessalians and Andrians on the ground that men are scarce, you cannot but refuse to put acknowledged citizens to death, men who should serve you well, and who will have the opportunity of doing so,if they are willing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references a contemporary practice or decree where Athens, facing a shortage of men, extends citizenship and its associated rights to specific foreign groups (Thessalians and Andrians) to bolster the city's strength.

verbatim: When you are ready to extend civic rights to Thessalians and Andrians on the ground that men are scarce, you cannot but refuse to put acknowledged citizens to death, men who should serve you well, and who will have the opportunity of doing so,if they are willing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author highlights a norm or expectation that 'acknowledged citizens' should be preserved rather than put to death, especially when the city is in need of manpower. This points to a right to life or state preservation for the in-group of formal citizens.
